common.legalDisclaimer: ImmiFina is not a bank, licensed financial advisor, or legal service. All content is for educational purposes only. Consult a licensed financial professional for advice specific to your situation.
common.benefitsDisclaimer: Benefit eligibility estimates are approximate and based on general program guidelines. Eligibility varies by state and individual circumstances. Always verify with official government sources.
common.remittanceDisclaimer: Fees and exchange rates are illustrative examples only, not live quotes. Compare providers before you send money.
common.waitlistSuccessLine: You are on the list. We will email you when we launch.
common.waitlistEmailInvalid: Please enter a valid email address
common.waitlistErrorGeneric: Something went wrong. Please try again or email us at info@immifina.org
common.eduAckBody: ImmiFina is not a bank, licensed financial advisor, or legal service. Information on this site—including tools and the AI assistant—is for general education only. It is not legal, tax, or financial advice for your situation.
common.eduAckCheckbox: I understand that ImmiFina provides educational information only and is not a substitute for professional advice.
common.verificationEmailSent: Verification email sent — check your inbox.

disclaimer.general: This is educational information only, not financial or legal advice. Consult a qualified professional before making decisions specific to your situation. Many community organizations offer free counseling for immigrants — see our resources page.
disclaimer.credit: This is educational information about credit. Not financial advice. Consider speaking with a nonprofit credit counselor for guidance specific to your situation.
disclaimer.tax: This is general tax education only, not tax advice. Tax situations vary. Free help is available through VITA (irs.gov/vita).
disclaimer.benefits: Benefit eligibility depends on many factors and changes frequently. Always verify with official sources. If you have an immigration case, consult an immigration attorney before applying for benefits.
disclaimer.banking: Banking requirements vary by institution and change frequently. Always verify directly with the bank or credit union.
disclaimer.remittance: Exchange rates and fees change constantly. Always verify the exact rate and fee at time of transfer on the provider's website.
disclaimer.legal: This is general educational information, not legal advice. Consult a qualified attorney for advice specific to your situation.

onboarding.title: Set up your profile
onboarding.intro: A few steps so we can personalize guidance, forecasts, and your next steps.
onboarding.s1Lead: What best describes your current status in the U.S.?
onboarding.optCitizen: U.S. citizen — you have full citizenship
onboarding.optGreen: Permanent resident (Green Card) — right to live and work permanently
onboarding.optVisa: Visa holder — here on a temporary visa (work, student, etc.)
onboarding.optDaca: DACA recipient — Deferred Action for Childhood Arrivals
onboarding.hasSsn: I have a Social Security Number (SSN)
onboarding.whatSsnBody: A Social Security number is a 9-digit ID used for work, taxes, and credit in the U.S. Not everyone has one right away.
onboarding.whatItinBody: An Individual Taxpayer Identification Number lets you file taxes without an SSN. It is not a work authorization by itself.
onboarding.yearsUs: How long have you lived in the U.S.?
onboarding.s2Lead: What is the most important financial thing you want to do right now?
onboarding.s4Lead: Monthly amounts help us personalize forecasts and tips. Estimates are OK.
onboarding.incomeHint: Money that actually hits your account after deductions. If you are not paid monthly, estimate a monthly amount: weekly — hourly × hours per week × 4.3; every two weeks — one paycheck × 2.17; twice per month — one paycheck × 2.
onboarding.canSave: I can save this much per month

landing.heroTitle: Your financial future, in plain language
landing.heroSubtitle: Understand your money and what to do next — built for immigrants and first-generation Americans navigating the U.S. financial system for the first time.
landing.testimonial1Quote: I can finally walk my parents through remittances and credit without a dozen browser tabs.
landing.testimonial2Quote: The paycheck and tax vocabulary made my first W-2 year less scary.
landing.testimonial3Quote: Having Spanish in the same product as the forecast keeps our household on the same page.
landing.problemTitle: Most finance apps were built for someone else
landing.problemBody: They assume years of U.S. credit history, fluent financial English, and products you already recognize. ImmiFina meets you where you are — with forward-looking tools and patient explanations.
landing.featForecastLead: Forecasts, guides, and remittance tools in one view — clear next steps, no tab sprawl.
landing.featBulletForecastDesc: Project savings with conservative assumptions and see milestones ahead.
landing.featBulletCreditDesc: How scores work and a gentle simulator — no hard pull.
landing.featBulletPaycheckDesc: Typical deductions, W-4, W-2 — educational only.
landing.featBulletBenefitsDesc: Programs to explore with links to official sources.
landing.featBulletBankingDesc: Checking, savings, and what to compare at banks or credit unions.
landing.featBulletRemitDesc: Compare fees and speeds across major providers.
landing.featForecastDesc: Project savings with conservative assumptions and see milestones ahead.
landing.featCreditDesc: Understand how scores work and explore a gentle simulator — no hard pull.
landing.featBenefitsDesc: See programs you may want to research further, with links to official sources.
landing.featRemitDesc: Compare representative fees and speeds across major providers.
landing.featPaycheckDesc: See what typical deductions mean on a U.S. paycheck — withholding, Social Security, Medicare, and forms like W-4 and W-2 (educational only).
landing.featBankingDesc: Learn how checking and savings work, what documents people often need by status, and what to compare when choosing a bank or credit union.
landing.featResourcesDesc: Free national helplines and directories for credit counseling, tax help (VITA), benefits, immigration legal aid, and financial education.
landing.trustLead: We know immigrants and first-generation families are careful with personal information — so we’re transparent about how ImmiFina works.
landing.trustDataBody: We use secure sign-in and encryption in line with common industry practice. You choose what you enter; we use it only to power your forecasts, guidance, and assistant experience in the app.
landing.trustNoBankBody: You never have to link a bank account to use ImmiFina. There’s no Plaid or read-only bank login in v1 — only what you choose to type in.
landing.trustNoSellBody: We don’t sell your personal information to data brokers or advertisers. If we offer optional services later, they won’t depend on selling your profile.
landing.howLead: One profile, then explore tools at your pace — finish with the assistant in the language you choose.
landing.how1Body: Income, expenses, and goals stay private; we use them to personalize guidance.
landing.how2Body: Forecasts and guides translate complex systems into clear next steps.
landing.how3Body: Chat in English, Spanish, or Chinese (Simplified) for calm, educational answers.
landing.langBody: English, Español, and 中文 (简体) at launch — same product, your choice.
landing.ctaBannerBody: We're building carefully. Leave your email for product news.

auth.name: Full name
auth.forgotBody: Password reset by email is not enabled in this preview. If you need access, register a new account or contact support.
auth.invalidCredentialsHint: If login works on your computer but not here, Vercel must use the same Supabase URL and service role key as your local .env.local — or create a new account on this site.
auth.authDbError: Could not read the user database. Check Supabase keys in Vercel (no extra spaces) and redeploy.
auth.authSecretMissing: Add AUTH_SECRET in Vercel → Environment Variables (a long random string, 32+ characters), save for Production, then redeploy. Without it, sign-in cannot set a session cookie.
auth.registerDbError: Could not create your account in the database. Open Supabase → SQL and run the script from supabase/schema.sql, then try again.
auth.serviceUnavailable: The app can't reach the database. In production, add NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL and SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY in Vercel → Settings → Environment Variables, redeploy, and ensure your Supabase SQL schema is applied.
auth.rateLimited: Too many attempts. Please wait and try again.
auth.emailTaken: An account with this email already exists.
auth.passwordRules: At least 8 characters with uppercase, lowercase, and a number.
auth.agreeToTerms: By creating an account, you agree to our <terms>Terms of Service</terms> and <privacy>Privacy Policy</privacy>.
auth.forgotSuccess: If an account with that email exists, we sent a password reset link. Check your inbox.
auth.forgotError: Something went wrong. Please try again.
auth.forgotRateLimited: Too many attempts. Please wait an hour and try again.
auth.checkEmailBody: We sent a verification link to your email address. Click it to verify your account. You can start using ImmiFina now.
auth.resetPasswordSuccess: Password updated. You can now log in.
auth.resetPasswordInvalidToken: This link is invalid or has expired. Please request a new one.
auth.resetPasswordError: Something went wrong. Please try again.
auth.verifyEmailSuccess: Email verified! You can now use your account.
auth.verifyEmailInvalid: This link is invalid or has already been used.
auth.verifyEmailExpired: This link has expired. Request a new one from your dashboard.

dashboard.journeyLead: Here's where you are on your financial journey.
dashboard.foundationHint: Educational score from your saved profile — not a credit or bank rating.
dashboard.foundationHowIntro: We add up to 100 points from items in your profile. Update anything in Settings.
dashboard.foundationPt2: 25 points — monthly income is set and monthly expenses are entered
dashboard.foundationPt3: 15 points — current savings amount is entered
dashboard.foundationPt4: 15 points — a main financial goal is selected
dashboard.foundationPt5: 10 points — state of residence is set
dashboard.foundationPt6: 5 points — you noted an SSN or ITIN in Settings
dashboard.foundationPt7: Up to 5 points — emergency savings vs one month of expenses (we scale savings ÷ expenses, up to 5 points when savings cover a full month)
dashboard.nextStepsLead: Based on your situation, here is what to explore first.
dashboard.surplusWarning: Your expenses are higher than income in this snapshot. Review categories in Forecast or ask the assistant for budgeting ideas.
dashboard.quickForecast: In 5 years, your savings could be about
dashboard.quickForecastNote: (4% annual growth, surplus saved monthly — educational estimate).
dashboard.emergencyBody: First milestone: save one month of expenses ({amount}).
dashboard.noChats: No conversations yet — start one from AI Assistant.
dashboard.situationHint: If your immigration status or main financial goal changes, update them in Settings — your next steps and guidance will adjust.

forecast.title: Financial forecast
forecast.hook: See where savings could go — from the numbers you enter below.
forecast.subtitle: Educational only, not a guarantee.
forecast.insightSavingsRate: How much you save each month usually matters more than small differences in return assumptions early on.
forecast.insightDebtFirst: You entered debt — use the “debt first” tab on the chart to compare another path.
forecast.nextStep1: Change income, expenses, or savings above and watch the chart update.
forecast.nextStep2: Update your profile in Settings if your situation changed.
forecast.nextStep3: Ask the AI assistant for ideas based on your inputs — or see free counseling on the Resources page.
forecast.understandIncome: Your monthly income here is what you actually receive after taxes — what lands in your account.
forecast.payFrequencyLead: If you are not paid once a month, estimate a monthly number for this page using one of these:
forecast.payFrequencyWeekly: Weekly: (hourly pay × hours you work per week) × 4.3 ≈ one month’s income.
forecast.payFrequencyBiweekly: Every two weeks (biweekly): one paycheck × 2.17 ≈ one month’s income.
forecast.payFrequencySemiMonthly: Twice per month (semi-monthly): one paycheck × 2 = one month’s income (two paydays per month).
forecast.budgetingLead: Pick what fits your culture and cash flow. These are common frameworks—not rules you must follow.
forecast.budgeting503020: 50/30/20 — split after-tax income roughly into needs (~50%), wants (~30%), and savings or debt payoff (~20%). Adjust the percentages if rent or remittances are high.
forecast.budgetingEnvelope: Envelope method — set a cash amount per category (rent, food, remittance) and stop spending when the envelope is empty. Digital apps can mimic this with separate balances.
forecast.budgetingPayFirst: Pay yourself first — on payday, move savings or emergency money to a separate account before you spend on discretionary items.
forecast.budgetingZero: Zero-based plan — every dollar is assigned a job (rent, food, savings, remittance) so income minus planned expenses equals zero. Helps when income varies week to week.
forecast.budgetingTailored: If you send money home regularly, treat remittance as a fixed “need” in your plan so other categories stay realistic.
forecast.budgetingDisc: Educational overview only. Not tax or legal advice.
forecast.expenseBreakdownLead: Splitting expenses shows where money goes. Totals update automatically.
forecast.saveRuleBody: A common guideline is 50/30/20 — needs, wants, then savings/debt. Even small consistent amounts help.
forecast.twentyPctLine: 20% of your income would be about {amount}/month — use the slider for what feels realistic.
forecast.sliderSaveLabel: I can save this much per month
forecast.forecastExplainBody: We use a conservative 4% annual return and your chosen monthly savings. The gray line shows growth if you add $0 per month (only your starting balance compounds).
forecast.chartSaving: If you save this amount monthly
forecast.chartTarget: If you hit your monthly target
forecast.milestoneEnd: After {years} years at this pace, about {amount} saved.
forecast.milestoneEmergency: Emergency fund (3 months expenses): about {months} months to reach at your surplus pace.
forecast.milestoneDown: Illustrative 20% down on a $400k home ($80k): about {years} years at your surplus pace.
forecast.msEmergency: Emergency fund (3× monthly expenses ≈ {amount}): ~{months} months to reach at this save rate.
forecast.ms5k: $5,000 saved milestone: ~{months} months at this pace.
forecast.msDown: Illustrative 10% down payment target ({amount}): ~{years} years at this pace (median home varies by state).
forecast.aiHint: We send your inputs to our assistant for educational ideas — not investment advice.
forecast.financialInlineDisclaimer: This is educational information only, not financial or legal advice. Consult a qualified professional before making decisions specific to your situation. Many community organizations offer free counseling for immigrants — see our resources page.
forecast.incomeSameQuestion: Is your income the same every month?
forecast.incomeVariesIntro: That is very common — many people are paid hourly, do gig work, or have seasonal jobs where income changes month to month. For planning purposes, it is often helpful to base your budget on your lowest typical month. That way your plan works even when times are slower.
forecast.incomeVariesPlanNote: We build your forecast using your lower number as the base. This is a conservative approach — if you earn more, you will simply be ahead of your plan.
forecast.incomeSmoothingNote: Some people also set aside extra money from high-income months into a separate account to cover lower-income months. This is sometimes called an "income smoothing" strategy.
forecast.currentDebtHint: Credit cards, loans, and other balances — estimate is OK.
forecast.tabDebtFocus: If I focus on debt first
forecast.tabSaveHint: Shows your current projection: savings grow from your starting balance plus monthly savings (4% annual return illustration).
forecast.tabDebtHint: Applies your monthly surplus to debt first (illustrative 20% APR on debt), then grows savings at 4% after the balance is paid off. Compare with the other tab using the same time horizon.

debtExplainer.lead: Something worth understanding before looking at your forecast:
debtExplainer.intro: You have entered {amount} in debt. Many financial educators discuss a common tradeoff between paying down debt and saving money.
debtExplainer.mathLead: Here is how the math works in your situation (illustrative rates):
debtExplainer.debtLine: If your debt has a {rate}% interest rate: every $100 you owe costs you about ${cost} per year in interest.
debtExplainer.saveLine: If you put $100 in a savings account at {rate}%: that $100 earns about ${earn} per year.
debtExplainer.pathA: This means some people with high-interest debt choose to pay it down first before saving — because the "cost" of the debt is higher than what savings earns.
debtExplainer.pathB: Others choose to do both at the same time — pay some debt and save a small amount — so they build the habit of saving while also reducing what they owe.
debtExplainer.noSingleAnswer: There is no single right answer. Below we show you two possible paths so you can see what each looks like for your situation.
debtExplainer.warn: This is educational information, not financial advice. A nonprofit credit counselor can help you think through your specific situation for free.

credit.title: Credit guide
credit.phase_no_historyBody: No U.S. score yet is normal for many newcomers. You are not “bad” — you are not on file yet.
credit.phase_buildingBody: You have started establishing accounts. On-time payments matter most now.
credit.phase_establishingBody: You have a track record. Keep utilization low and avoid missed payments.
credit.phase_optimizingBody: You are in a stronger position — focus on rates, mix, and long-term habits.
credit.focusFirst_no_history:
Open one starter account you can pay on time, every time.
Keep balances low compared to limits when you have revolving credit.
Avoid applying for many cards at once — spacing matters.
credit.focusFirst_building:
Pay at least the minimum on time — late payments hurt the most.
Keep credit use under roughly 30% of limits if you can.
Let accounts age — length of history helps.
credit.focusFirst_establishing:
Continue on-time payments and low utilization.
Avoid closing old accounts without a reason — age helps.
Check your free reports for errors once you’re comfortable.
credit.focusFirst_optimizing:
Shop rates on loans when your score supports it.
Keep a healthy mix without borrowing just for “variety.”
Plan ahead before large applications (car, apartment).
credit.actionNext1: Try the score range simulator below — educational only.
credit.actionNext2: Read nonprofit or CFPB guidance before applying for products.
credit.actionNext3: Consider free credit counseling if you want a plan for debt.
credit.whatBody: A credit score is a number lenders use to estimate how likely you are to repay debt. It is built from your credit report — not from income or savings.
credit.whatBodyExtended: Scores usually range from 300–850. Think of it as a reputation signal for borrowing — not a judgment of your worth.
credit.rangePoor: 300–579: Poor — harder to rent or borrow affordably.
credit.rangeFair: 580–669: Fair — some options, often higher rates.
credit.rangeGood: 670–739: Good — many lenders will work with you.
credit.rangeVg: 740–799: Very Good — better rates and approvals.
credit.invisible: When you first arrive you may be “credit invisible” — no U.S. file yet. That is common and fixable with the right first products.
credit.whyImmigrantBody: Without a U.S. score, renting, phone plans, and loans can be harder — even if you were financially responsible abroad. U.S. history does not transfer.
credit.guidedLead: Answer a few plain questions — we map to a rough educational range, not your real score.
credit.gqAccounts: Do you have any U.S. credit accounts right now?
credit.gqOldest: How long have you had your oldest U.S. credit account?
credit.gqMissed: Have you ever missed a payment in the U.S.?
credit.gqPayFull: Do you pay credit card balances in full each month?
credit.gqPayNa: I don't have a card yet
credit.gqInq: Hard credit applications in the last 6 months?
credit.guidedDisclaimer: Your real score from a bureau may differ. This is not a credit pull.
credit.payment: Payment history — paying on time matters most.
credit.utilization: Credit utilization — how much of your limit you use.
credit.age: Length of credit history — older accounts help when managed well.
credit.mix: Credit mix — a healthy blend of cards and installment loans.
credit.inquiries: New credit — many hard inquiries in a short window can lower scores.
credit.assessBody: This is a self-check, not a score pull. Honest answers help you pick the right first steps.
credit.planLead: Tap a section to open plain-language definitions for any unfamiliar words.
credit.planCitizenAction: Consider a no-annual-fee card, autopay, and keeping utilization low.
credit.planVisaAction: Confirm issuer policies; secured cards and credit-builder products are common starting points.
credit.planOtherAction: Focus on records you can document and products designed for thin or no credit file.
credit.planTermAnnualFee_def: Some cards charge a yearly cost just to keep the account open. “No annual fee” means that yearly cost is $0.
credit.planTermAutopay_def: Automatic payment from your bank so your bill is paid on time—helps avoid missed payments.
credit.planTermUtilization_def: How much of your credit limit you use. Using a small share of your limit is usually better for scores.
credit.planTermIssuer_def: The bank or company that offers the card (for example, a national bank, a credit union, or a card brand). Rules can vary by issuer.
credit.planTermSecured_def: You put down a refundable deposit; your limit is often close to that amount. It is a common way to start U.S. credit history.
credit.planTermCreditBuilder_def: A small loan or account meant to report on-time payments to credit bureaus so your credit file grows.
credit.planTermDocument_def: Things like pay stubs, rent history, bank statements, or tax ID paperwork—proof lenders may use to understand your situation.
credit.planTermThinFile_def: Few or no accounts on your U.S. credit report—or none yet. That is common for newcomers; some products are built for this.
credit.stepSecured: Secured credit card — refundable deposit, lower risk for the issuer.
credit.stepBuilder: Credit-builder loan — small installment reported to bureaus.
credit.stepAU: Authorized user — if a trusted person adds you responsibly to their account.
credit.simHint: Adjust sliders to see a rough educational range — not your real score.
credit.qNew: Are you new to U.S. credit?
credit.qSecured: Have you opened a secured card or credit-builder product?

emotional.body:
If you had a bank account or credit history in your home country, that history unfortunately does not transfer to the United States.

This affects virtually every immigrant. It is one of the most frustrating parts of starting life in a new country — and it is not your fault.

You are not starting over because you failed. You are starting over because the system was not built to recognize your history.

ImmiFina was built to help you navigate this.

itinPath.title: Building credit with an ITIN
itinPath.intro: If you do not yet have a Social Security Number, you may still be able to start building US credit history using your ITIN (Individual Taxpayer Identification Number).
itinPath.important: Important: Not all banks and credit card issuers accept ITIN. The options below are commonly reported to accept ITIN — however, policies change and vary by location. Always confirm directly with the institution before applying.
itinPath.optionsTitle: Options commonly explored by immigrants with an ITIN:
itinPath.opt1Title: Secured credit cards that may accept ITIN
itinPath.opt1Body:
Some issuers that have been reported to accept ITIN include:
• OpenSky Secured Visa — does not require SSN or bank account
• Self Credit Builder — reports to all 3 credit bureaus
• Some local credit unions — policies vary by institution

Note: We are not recommending or endorsing any specific product. Research current terms and requirements before applying.
itinPath.opt2Body:
Many community credit unions specifically serve immigrants and may accept ITIN for account opening and credit products.
Common search terms to find one near you:
"immigrant-friendly credit union [your city]" or
"ITIN credit union [your state]"
itinPath.opt3Body:
Nova Credit is a service that can import credit history from some countries directly to certain US lenders.
Countries currently supported include: Mexico, India, UK, Canada, Australia, Brazil, Dominican Republic, Kenya, Nigeria, Philippines, South Korea, Spain, Switzerland.

This may be worth exploring if your country is on this list and you have an established credit history from home.
itinPath.opt4Body:
If you have a trusted family member or friend with a US credit card account in good standing, being added as an authorized user on their account may help you begin building credit history — even with just an ITIN.

This requires significant trust between both parties. The primary cardholder is responsible for all charges.
itinPath.warn: Educational note: Credit policies change frequently. Always verify current requirements directly with any institution before applying. Applying for credit you are unlikely to be approved for can result in hard inquiries that temporarily lower your score.

predatory.intro:
When you are new to the US credit system and have no credit history yet, some lenders may offer you products with very high costs. Understanding what these are can help you make more informed decisions.

The following are educational descriptions of financial products that are commonly discussed as high-cost options:
predatory.paydayBody: These are short-term loans typically due on your next payday. They often carry fees equivalent to annual interest rates of 300–400% or more. For example, a $300 payday loan with a $45 fee due in 2 weeks has an equivalent annual rate of approximately 391%.
predatory.noCreditBody: Some lenders specifically target people with no credit history. These often carry annual interest rates of 100–400%. The absence of a credit check typically means significantly higher costs to the borrower.
predatory.rentToOwnBody: These allow you to take home items immediately with weekly or monthly payments. The total cost over the payment period often significantly exceeds the retail price of the item. A $400 television may cost $1,200–1,600 total in a rent-to-own arrangement.
predatory.repairTitle: "Credit repair" services that charge upfront fees
predatory.repairBody: No service can legally remove accurate negative information from your credit report. Anything accurate stays on your report for the legally defined time period regardless of what you pay. You have the right to dispute inaccurate information yourself for free at annualcreditreport.com.
predatory.principle: A general principle many financial educators suggest: If an annual interest rate is above 30%, it may be worth exploring whether alternative options exist before proceeding.
predatory.warn: This is educational information only. If you are considering any financial product and are unsure about the terms, a nonprofit credit counselor can review it with you at no cost.

benefits.title: Benefits finder
benefits.subtitle: Explore programs that match your situation. Always confirm with official sources.
benefits.privacyLead: Your answers stay private and are used only to suggest programs. We do not verify immigration status — this is educational.
benefits.hook: Explore programs you may qualify for — then confirm with official sources.
benefits.reassuranceTitle: You may not need to worry about everything you read online
benefits.reassuranceBody: Many people in these situations explore benefits without the issues they fear: U.S. citizens, many permanent residents, and people with no active immigration application. Rules still depend on your case.
benefits.bucketsTitle: Three ways to think about it
benefits.bucketSafeBody: Children’s benefits, emergency Medicaid, and many nutrition programs — verify state rules.
benefits.bucketDependsBody: Some cash and health programs may matter for certain green card applications.
benefits.bucketExpertBody: If you have an active or upcoming case, many advocates suggest an accredited representative or attorney before applying.
benefits.publicChargeTitle: Will applying for benefits affect my immigration case?
benefits.publicChargeLead: This is a common question. The answer depends on your situation. This is general education — not legal advice.
benefits.publicChargeBody:
This is one of the most common questions immigrants have, and the answer is complicated. Here is general educational information — immigration law is complex and changes frequently, so this should not be taken as legal advice.

General information (as of 2024):
• Benefits used by your US-born children generally do not count against a parent's immigration case
• Emergency Medicaid is generally not counted
• SNAP, non-emergency Medicaid, and housing assistance may be considered in some green card applications under what is called the "public charge" rule
• This rule generally applies only to those applying for a green card (lawful permanent residence) from inside the US, or applying at a US consulate abroad
• It generally does not apply to refugees, asylees, VAWA applicants, or certain other categories

Because immigration law is complex and consequences can be significant, many immigration advocates recommend consulting with an accredited immigration representative or attorney before applying for certain benefits if you have an active or upcoming immigration case.
benefits.publicChargeFooter: If you do not have an active immigration case, or if you are a US citizen, these concerns may not apply to you.
benefits.publicChargeAck: I understand — show me programs I may qualify for
benefits.nextStep1: Confirm rules on official state or federal sites before you apply.
benefits.nextStep2: Use free tax help (VITA) if you are claiming credits or unsure about forms.
benefits.nextStep3: If you have an active immigration case, talk to an accredited attorney before applying for certain benefits.
benefits.earnedIncomeLabel: Do you have earned income from work (not only investments)?
benefits.eitcTitle: You may qualify for a significant tax refund
benefits.eitcLead: The Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) is a refund from the federal government for people who work and earn below certain income limits.
benefits.eitcWhatBody: The government returns a portion of the taxes you paid throughout the year. For many working families, this is the largest single payment they receive all year.
benefits.eitcEstimate: Estimated maximum credit for your inputs (illustrative): household of {household}, income ${income}: up to ${max} back at tax time
benefits.eitcWho1: You must have earned income (from work, not investments)
benefits.eitcWho2: Income must be below the limit for your household size
benefits.eitcWho3: You must have a valid SSN (or your children must)
benefits.eitcWho4: ITIN holders: you may qualify if your children have SSNs
benefits.eitcVita: How to claim it — for free:
benefits.eitcVitaBody: VITA (Volunteer Income Tax Assistance) offers free tax filing help at community locations across the US, with multilingual support available in many languages. This service is offered by IRS-certified volunteers and is completely free for people who qualify.
benefits.eitcVitaLink: Find a VITA location near you
benefits.eitcEsLink: Find free tax help in Spanish
benefits.eitcWarn: Tax situations vary. This estimate is based on general EITC tables and your inputs. A VITA volunteer can give you an accurate picture based on your full tax situation.
benefits.pregnantHint: Some programs focus on pregnant people and new parents.

eligibility.high: Strong match — worth reviewing official rules
eligibility.medium: Possible match — confirm with your state
eligibility.low: Less likely — still check if circumstances apply

medicaid.name: Medicaid / CHIP
medicaid.covers: Health coverage for low-income adults, children, pregnant women, elderly, and people with disabilities.
medicaid.elig: Often tied to Federal Poverty Level (FPL) and state rules.
medicaid.lang: Many states offer interpreter services; check your state Medicaid site.

snap.name: SNAP (food assistance)
snap.covers: Monthly benefits for groceries through an EBT card.
snap.elig: Income and asset tests vary by state; work rules may apply.
snap.lang: USDA and state sites offer multiple languages.

wic.name: WIC
wic.covers: Nutrition support for pregnant women, new parents, and young children.
wic.elig: Income at or below 185% FPL and nutritional risk criteria.
wic.lang: State WIC agencies provide multilingual materials.

liheap.name: LIHEAP
liheap.covers: Help with home energy bills and weatherization in some cases.
liheap.elig: Income guidelines set by state; funding is limited.
liheap.lang: Contact your state LIHEAP office for application help.

section8.name: Section 8 / Housing Choice Vouchers
section8.elig: Income limits vary by area and household size.
section8.lang: HUD and local Public Housing Agencies provide guidance.

headstart.name: Head Start / Early Head Start
headstart.covers: Early learning, health, and family support for young children.
headstart.elig: Generally for families at or below 100% FPL or receiving certain benefits.
headstart.lang: Programs emphasize family engagement in home languages.

schoolmeals.name: Free and reduced-price school meals
schoolmeals.covers: Breakfast and lunch at school for eligible students.
schoolmeals.elig: Household income or participation in certain programs.
schoolmeals.lang: Your school district handles applications — ask for translated forms.

remittance.title: Remittance comparison
remittance.subtitle: Illustrative comparison across major providers — verify before you send.
remittance.introBody: Sending money home is a regular decision for many families. Small differences in fees and exchange rates add up over a year — compare what your family actually receives, not just the “fee.”
remittance.bankHelp: Bank transfer: best when your family has a bank account; often cheaper for larger amounts; may take a few days.
remittance.cashHelp: Cash pickup: fast and widely available; your family picks up cash with ID; fees can be higher.
remittance.walletHelp: Mobile wallet: instant in some countries; requires the right app on your family’s phone.
remittance.tip1: Compare before every transfer — rates change daily.
remittance.tip2: Watch “$0 fee” offers — the exchange rate may be worse.
remittance.tip3: Larger amounts sometimes get better rates.
remittance.tip4: Weekend transfers can have worse rates.
remittance.tip5: Keep records for taxes and your own notes.
remittance.tip6: Recurring transfers may earn loyalty discounts with some providers.
remittance.timingTitle: When might be a good time to send
remittance.timingBody:
Exchange rates change constantly — every day and even throughout the day. No one can predict exactly when rates will be best. However, some patterns that financial educators note:

• Rates can sometimes be less favorable on Mondays, when currency markets reopen after the weekend
• Mid-week days (Tuesday through Thursday) have historically shown more stability in some corridors
• Major holidays in either the US or your destination country can affect rates and processing times
• Some providers offer promotional rates periodically — worth checking their apps directly

The most reliable approach many people use: Compare rates across 2–3 providers each time you send, rather than always using the same one. A few minutes of comparison can sometimes save $10–20 per transfer.
remittance.timingNote: Past rate patterns do not predict future rates. Always verify the exact rate at the time of your transfer.
remittance.recurringBody:
If you send money to family on a regular schedule — every week, every two weeks, or every month — setting up a recurring transfer may be worth considering.

Potential benefits some providers offer for recurring transfers:
• Reduced fees after a certain number of transfers
• Ability to lock in a rate for a period of time (varies by provider)
• Automatic scheduling so you do not need to remember each time
• Some providers send notifications to recipients automatically

How to set up recurring transfers — general steps:
1. Create an account with your chosen provider
2. Verify your identity (typically requires a government ID)
3. Add your recipient's bank account or payment details
4. Choose "recurring" or "scheduled" transfer option
5. Set the frequency and amount

Providers that offer recurring/scheduled transfer options (verify current availability on their website):
• Wise — offers scheduled transfers
• Remitly — offers AutoSend
• WorldRemit — offers recurring transfers
• Western Union — offers recurring option in some corridors
remittance.recurringNote: Features and availability change. Always verify current options directly with the provider.

paycheck.title: Understanding your US paycheck
paycheck.hook: Adjust inputs — your estimated take-home updates instantly (illustrative, not tax advice).
paycheck.subtitle: See what typical deductions mean. Educational only.
paycheck.heroHighlight: Take-home is about {pct}% of gross pay (illustrative).
paycheck.interactionHint: Try changing gross pay, state, or filing status.
paycheck.grossHelp: Your employer pays this total amount. Deductions are taken from this to arrive at your take-home pay.
paycheck.stateLabel: Which state do you work in?
paycheck.stateHelp: Each state has different income tax rates. Some states have no income tax at all.
paycheck.filingHelp: This tells the government roughly how much to withhold from each paycheck. If you are unsure, "Single" is a common starting point for new workers.
paycheck.depLabel: Number of dependents (children or others you support)
paycheck.healthLabel: Other: health insurance (per month, if known)
paycheck.stubFederalInfo: Tax collected by the US federal government. The amount depends on your income level and filing status.
paycheck.stubStateInfo: Tax collected by your state. Several states have no state income tax.
paycheck.stubSsInfo: 6.2% of your gross pay goes to the Social Security system. This funds retirement benefits for current retirees and builds your own record for future benefits. Note for immigrants: Even if you are uncertain whether you will retire in the US, you may be building Social Security credits. After 40 credits (typically 10 years of work), you may be eligible for benefits. Your credits are tied to your SSN.
paycheck.stubMedInfo: 1.45% of your gross pay funds Medicare, the federal health insurance program for people 65 and older. Note: Even if you are not a US citizen, if you work with valid work authorization, these contributions go toward your record.
paycheck.stubPct: This is approximately {pct}% of your gross pay.
paycheck.formsW4: W-4 (Employee's Withholding Certificate) — tells your employer how much federal tax to withhold. You typically fill it when you start a job or when your situation changes. Your employer provides it; the IRS also has it at irs.gov/w4. If you fill it out incorrectly, you may owe money at tax time or get a smaller refund. Many people start with the basic default settings.
paycheck.formsW2: W-2 (Wage and Tax Statement) — summary of earnings and taxes withheld for the year. Employers send it by January 31. You use it to file your annual tax return.
paycheck.forms1099: 1099 (for self-employed or gig workers) — if you work as a contractor or receive non-employee income, you may receive a 1099 instead of a W-2. No taxes are withheld automatically; you are responsible for setting aside money. Many tax educators suggest setting aside 25–30% of 1099 income as a starting point — your actual amount depends on your full situation.
paycheck.formsVita: Tax situations vary widely. VITA offers free tax help and can review your specific situation.
paycheck.faqRefund: Can I get any of this money back? Federal and state income tax: Possibly, yes — when you file your annual return. Social Security and Medicare: Generally no — these are contributions to those systems.
paycheck.faqMultiJob: What if I work multiple jobs? Each employer withholds as if that job is your only income. You may need to adjust withholding — a VITA volunteer can help.
paycheck.faqCash: What if my employer pays me in cash? You are still generally required to report income. This can be complex — speaking with a VITA volunteer may help.
paycheck.stubWarn: All figures shown are estimates for educational purposes. Your actual paycheck will differ based on your W-4, employer deductions, and other factors. This is not tax advice.
paycheck.nextStep1: If withholding feels too high or low, ask HR about your W-4 or visit a VITA site.
paycheck.nextStep2: Check your state tax agency’s site for rules where you work.
paycheck.nextStep3: Use the AI assistant if a line on your pay stub is confusing (educational only).

banking.title: Banking Guide
banking.hook: Pick what to optimize for — then compare real banks and credit unions where you live.
banking.subtitle: An educational overview of opening a first US bank account — not an endorsement of any institution.
banking.decisionLowFeesHint: Many people compare online banks and larger ATM networks.
banking.decisionInPersonHint: A local credit union or community branch can be easier for questions.
banking.decisionSecondChanceHint: Some banks offer “second chance” or starter accounts — policies vary by place.
banking.decisionNote: Not a recommendation. Always verify requirements before you apply.
banking.nextStep1: Call or visit two institutions and ask about fees, minimums, and ID requirements.
banking.nextStep2: Bring the documents that match your situation (see above).
banking.nextStep3: Ask about overdraft options you can turn off if you want fewer surprise fees.
banking.s1Body:
Checking account — for everyday spending: deposits, debit card, checks, transfers. Look for no monthly fee or a fee waived with direct deposit.

Savings account — for money you are not spending immediately; earns a small amount of interest. Look for a competitive APY and no minimum balance.

Credit union vs. bank — credit unions are nonprofit cooperatives owned by members; many have lower fees and serve immigrant communities.

Online vs. traditional — online banks often have lower fees; traditional banks have branches.
banking.s2Citizen: If you are a U.S. citizen or permanent resident: government photo ID, SSN, proof of address, and a starting deposit (often $0–100 depending on the bank).
banking.s2Visa: If you are a visa holder: passport with valid visa, ITIN or SSN (some banks require one), proof of US address; some banks may ask for visa expiration. Not all banks accept visa holders — calling ahead helps.
banking.s2Other: If you have no SSN or limited documentation: some banks and credit unions accept a foreign passport plus proof of address; an ITIN may work at some institutions. Search "ITIN bank account" or "immigrant-friendly bank account" for current options in your area.
banking.s2Note: Banking policies change frequently. Always verify requirements directly before visiting.
banking.s3Title: What to look for — and what to watch out for
banking.s3Body:
Look for: no monthly fee (or easy waiver), no punishing minimum balance, free ATM access or fee reimbursement, multilingual support.

Watch out for: overdraft fees ($25–35 per occurrence in many cases), high minimum opening deposits, paper statement fees, and high wire fees for international transfers — remittance services are often cheaper for sending money abroad.
banking.s4Body: Many banks check ChexSystems when you apply — similar to a credit report for banking history. If you are new to the US, you may have no record (often fine). If denied, ask why; you can request a free report at chexsystems.com. Some banks offer "second chance" accounts.
banking.s5Body: CDFIs serve underserved communities. Immigrant-serving credit unions exist in many cities. Online banks sometimes have minimal requirements. The app does not maintain a list of specific banks — policies change. Our AI assistant can help you think through questions to ask when you contact a bank.

resources.title: Free resources for immigrants
resources.body:
Financial counseling (free):
• NFCC — nonprofit credit counseling — nfcc.org | 1-800-388-2227
• CFPB — consumerfinance.gov (Spanish available)

Tax help (free):
• VITA — irs.gov/vita
• Tax Aide (AARP) — aarp.org/taxaide

Benefits help (free):
• Benefits.gov
• 211 — call or text for local social services

Immigration legal help (free or low cost):
• ImmigrationAdvocates.org
• CLINIC — cliniclegal.org
• AILA — aila.org

Banking help (free):
• CFPB "Your Money, Your Goals" — consumerfinance.gov/consumer-tools/financial-well-being

ImmiFina does not endorse or verify any of the above organizations. Always verify that an organization is legitimate before sharing personal information.

chat.title: AI Assistant
chat.empty: Start a conversation. Replies are educational, not personal financial advice.
chat.error: Could not get a reply. Try again.
chat.rateLimited: You're sending messages too quickly. Please wait a moment.
chat.historyEmpty: No saved chats yet. Send a message to start one.

settings.title: Settings
settings.name: Full name
settings.situationLead: Update when your situation changes — for example if you naturalize, get a green card, or shift your top financial priority.
